speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Johtje--

speaker: interviewee
text: Johtje Vos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vos. August the 13th, 1996. What I started to ask when our tape was ending was, with all these Jews there, were there any Jewish rituals performed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, among them, but I was never there. But they didn't do [INAUDIBLE] first of all, none of them was a religious Jew. They were all not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Secular Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I have never noticed much of that. But sometimes, they gathered together, and I let them alone. So probably they did. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The baby that was born, was it a girl or a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: A girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: A girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did not need to have a circumcision.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. In general, how would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides, circumcision was not done that much in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh, it wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many Jewish people were not circumcised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Your own children, you mentioned your mother and father did not know about the fact that you were hiding people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning. But one day, my mother come and of course saw it. And was very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she agreed with the principle. But she said, you have your first responsibility to your children. And when they get you? I said, well, when they get us, they will certainly not take our children when they know that the children are German. And secondly, our principle was, if we are caught, they will probably not take the children. Or kill the children, certainly not. And then when the children grow up, it's better that they know that their parents have died for what they believed in, than that he used them as a pretext-- we can't do it because we have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother react to that statement?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't agree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father around then too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father had died in the beginning of the war already.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, did your children go to school during this time? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. First of all, they were very small. They were 2 and 4, or 3 and 5, something like that. No, they did not go to school. After the war, they went to school. And then we kept the Jewish girl. And then my very blonde, youngest daughter with this very dark Jewish girl, called themselves twins in school. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking-- I'm sorry I'm jumping around, but I did forget to ask you. Were-- with the group, were you trained in any way what to say in case of an interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in case you were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, there was no training whatsoever. But we talked about it [? among us ?]. And, for instance, when one had had a raid in the house, he said, well, what exactly did you say? And what did he ask and all? So we learned from each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was liberation for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 5th of May in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: '45. And what happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is that we yelled so loud for so long that for three days nobody could talk anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came in. First of all, we heard it. We had one-- one neighbor [INAUDIBLE] in our-- near our house who had a radio with whom he could hear England. And he heard that night. That happened late at night. And the Dutch prime minister in England was going to talk that morning. So he-- he invited us all to come, and we all sat on the bed of the family where they had beind the wall their secret radio. And we heard it. And we heard the prime minister in London, with-- with tears in his voice, keeps screaming [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I'll never forget this word.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you call people who live in the same country? Citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Patriots?

speaker: interviewer
text: Patriots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Patriots. Patriots, you are free. And he kept singing that, and yelling that, and screaming that, and crying that. And we all sat here, well, just totally-- totally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an army that liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Canadians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Canadian Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And later, the Americans too. But the first who came and liberated us were the Canadians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. They went through all the-- we lived very near the big road from Amsterdam to Germany. So we all stood for two days on that road and did nothing but, hurray, thank you brothers! You know, help us! You know? Crying, all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the people we--

speaker: interviewer
text: That were hidden-- at liberation. What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- They were standing with us on the road and yelling 'hurray'!

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To their eventual homes and houses. And we have a family, I don't know. We got in contact with most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who remained with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was with me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than Moana?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were lots of people with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That remained after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, after the war, nobody. They all went to their own places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thankfully, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were really the one who was taking care of Moana. How did-- How did that taking her back to the orphanage go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand what you mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you gave-- you brought Moana back to the orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I-- I didn't bring her back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: She had never been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. They-- they came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they came to get her.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew she was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah, we had given that up, of course, immediately. And then we had tried to find out if she had any family. And it proved that she had family in Indonesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we let them know that Moana was-- and I knew her family, so actually, her uncle in Indonesia was an old boyfriend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I immediately got in touch with him, as soon as we had a telephone and possibilities to reach other countries, and then I let him know that I had Moana. And so after that, they came and got her. Because it was very tragic, because the mother-- they were in Indonesia during the war and had no trouble, although from Japan, of course. And so after the war, the-- he-- he was a doctor. And he had done beautiful work in the camps where they were. His camp had the least dead people and the least sick people, because he took care of all the [INAUDIBLE] of them. And in thankfulness for that, his wife was allowed with the children to go on the first boat which came from Indonesia to Holland. But what was very tragic, on that boat, there was all kind of things lacking. And anyway, there was an epidemic of pneumonia, and two of her three children died. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So. Do you-- how much contact do you have with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them don't live anymore. I'm an old woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Most of them are not alive. Do you have any contact--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That family, she lives yet--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom do you have contact with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the mother of-- of that child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moana's mother survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moana's-- No, Moana's aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- aunt. Survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Moana herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [INAUDIBLE] I'm always in contact, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do these people-- did these people ever get together between the end of the war and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when we were of-- last year. Of-- Or was it beginning of this year? In-- in Holland. And a German-- a-- a journalist, a very known one, [INAUDIBLE] his name. Everybody in Europe knows his name. And [INAUDIBLE] came and [INAUDIBLE] had a little talk with me. And then he took the names of all the people who were still-- and then he one day phoned me and said to me, [INAUDIBLE] to have next week-- Wednesday or Thursday or Monday, a very light celebration. And he had invited them all. He had worked to find them back all who are still alive, and he gave a fantastic dinner party. So that was very moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you-- you and your-- you were there, not your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there. [INTERPOSING VOICES] My husband didn't live anymore, no. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You left Holland shortly after that, after the war. What happened to you after all these people left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was remarried. And-- and well, they all left, and we became a family again. Which of course was wonderful. Because, for instance, one of my daughters, my oldest daughter, still has-- has resentment about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we paid too much attention to all the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how does she--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does she--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she felt neglected, which she wasn't. But she felt that way. And my oldest-- my-- my younger daughter is-- is always very thankful and proud of the period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How else did this experience affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: How else did this whole experience affect your life? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's difficult to say, of course. First of all, I have been asked [INAUDIBLE] to speak-- to give speeches endlessly, which I finally stopped. I said, I'm just not going to do it anymore. I retire. But I have been in I don't know how many universities, and schools, and places all over America, to speak about all that happened in that time. Which I didn't like very much. But I did it, because they always said it was important and necessary and all. But I don't do that anymore. I find I'm too old. I don't have to anymore. I've done my duty. [LAUGHS] And for the rest, yeah. We never talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk-- do you talk about it with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never talked. No, we never did. And then-- well, the children knew, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what had happened. But we never talked about it. And then one day, my-- one of my sons found it so necessary. So one day he invited a group of Jewish friends, without knowing it, and he arranged a dinner party to be catered. And at 5 o'clock, I didn't know anything about that. And at 6 o'clock, I suddenly had a big dinner party of all those Jewish people. And my-- and he had rented an-- kind of a thing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: A VCR?

speaker: interviewee
text: And put it up. And he said, OK, now you people can ask questions. And I [INAUDIBLE] my husband and I didn't know what happened. And suddenly, we were in the middle there. So we were-- we started to tell. And then I was very often asked out to come and give speeches in many places, schools and universities. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the most difficult time for you-- in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the whole time was.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this period? In this period of the four years of hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't-- can't see what was the most difficult. The most difficult was of course when we had raids. They were the most difficult moments. And I found it always very difficult when the people among them not were nice to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you did what you did? What motivated you? Was there anything in your background that influenced you to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm-- I'm a religious woman. And I cannot-- I cannot say God [INAUDIBLE] or God told me. But I think it had something to do with that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything in your life, in your growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had Jewish friends. And I had this-- it started with that girl, because she was the-- the niece of-- of my old Jewish boyfriend. And [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Moana?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in [INAUDIBLE]. In Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but her name is Moana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moana, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I went there. And then-- and then we had friends who were Jewish, and [INAUDIBLE] in the beginning, before they all were caught, they were sent to live in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had made [INAUDIBLE] for the Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, so they came to us and said, we are so desperate because [INAUDIBLE]. We said, don't be desperate! Give them to us. And from there, it stated, you know? Yeah. But in your early life, as a child, or growing up as a teenager, was there something or someone who influenced you? That perhaps made you do what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my Christian upbringing I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Christian upbringing [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were always helping people. And, you know, and that-- that was something normal for us. When we saw somebody in difficulty, we helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get compensated for the people that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Never a penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Many people offered. We had one-- it was a professor from, a-- a Jewish professor from Amsterdam. [INAUDIBLE]. He had heard about us, I don't know how. And when he had to hide, he said to the group who were helping him, we want to go to [INAUDIBLE]. But he didn't even know us. And so they came to us. And he said, we have this couple, and they want to come to you. They refuse to go anywhere else. [INAUDIBLE] have to hide, we want to hide here. So we said, OK, let them come. And so that night, he came. He was [INAUDIBLE]. And he grabbed [INAUDIBLE] shirt, and he came [INAUDIBLE] full of [INAUDIBLE] things and [INAUDIBLE]. And he took it out, and he [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --catch, you know? He kept them [INAUDIBLE]. Take what you want. You can also take it all, because tomorrow I can have the same amount. I'm a very rich man. And we said no, sir. We don't want money. And he said, yes, but I will only come with money. I said, well, then certainly then you don't come. Because we do not want money for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never paid. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But one thing very funny happened, though. Which is not a nice story which I often tell. No, I don't even tell it to you, no. We once were in trouble, and he refused to help us. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever testify against any of these Germans who interrogated or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Yeah. But you have been honored by a lot of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have that whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know where it is, that whole thing for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the person, or the-- who are the people, who are submitting your name to be honored?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that-- that some friends from the war told it in Israel. And then we got in a thing from Israel. And that was known by all the organizations and all. We just got [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been honored by your own people? By the Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Dutch Jewish people, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Dutch-- Dutch Jewish people did honor you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then here in America, what did we get here again? We had a terrible, honorable thing. It was so funny, because we didn't realize that it was going to be so great. And my husband and I went, and it was raining a bit. And we first went shopping, you know? [LAUGHS] I wasn't even well dressed for it. And so then we came here, in the Israeli consulate, and there was a big thing for us. We had no idea. I wasn't dressed for it at all, you know. I just [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER] It was very funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Vos, what was your greatest satisfaction in saving--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that I could help young children-- people. That I had the possibility. I thanked God for that, [INAUDIBLE] that we had the possibility to help people. Because some people lived next to-- although we didn't trust our neighbors entirely. It proved later that they were trustworthy, but we were not sure. So that was a bit. But there were people who just couldn't do it, for all kind of family circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still helping people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still helping people in different ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, if I can. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your children? Have they--

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest--

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: --daughter has a resentment about that time. My youngest daughter is very happy that we did it. And my sons were born after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. But are they involved in social action of this sort, in helping people in distress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, my-- my oldest daughter is a nurse. And she helps a lot of people. And the youngest daughter is a lab technician. So they both are in the medical field, and they both help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they politically or socially involved in helping people? Like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, actually, we never talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why some things I have forgotten, because I have-- it's so long not talked about it. We never talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to add anything to this story? This testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I can say that I'm happy I did it. I've never regretted, in spite of all the difficulties which it gave us, I'm always happy when I think about that horrible time of that occupation of our country, that I could help. That I had a possibility. [INAUDIBLE] And I'm very thankful for the way God helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to show some of the pictures that you brought.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't listen to--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to show some of the pictures and some copies of some books that you have written.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You right now, are-- are you still writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still working as a journalist?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't do that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to show-- to show you-- show a few things. We're going to cut the tape now, and then we'll be showing the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. How long yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the house where we lived and where we went through all those experiences during the World War. That was a thatched roof. And it was not a very big house, but it was-- it had three or four bedrooms. And so we hid all the people in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who took that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my husband did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we obviously lived there yet, so it must have been before '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the children are quite little in that picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Those are the two girls who-- the eldest girl there is 60 years old now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. OK, tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's a picture taken in the-- in the dead end road where we lived, where our house was. And that was in the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- And my dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband-- And your husband's name, please? Your husband's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband names was Aart, A-A-R-T, Vos, V-O-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Tell us about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my husband, Aart, hidden in the coal bin which we had emptied out and made a false bottom. So that when you looked from the top, where the entrance was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where the entrance was, and he had made a double bottom in it, so that when you opened it from the top, which was the official opening, you saw coals and didn't know better. But then under that, he had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Just don't waive your hand so, because you're blocking the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I have to start all over?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just tell us what-- rest of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] a coal bin, which-- where in which my husband built a double bottom so that he could go in it, under it. Not only he, but all the people who had to hide. And when you opened it from the top, which was the official opening, then you saw coals and didn't expect that there was a whole hole under it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he built it by himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he rebuilt it. It was originally just a coal bin, but he rebuilt it with this whole contraption to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was at our neighbor's house, who did exactly the same as we did. And one of the boys had dug a big hole to fit in. And they show here on the picture how they got him out again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was David, but I don't remember his last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the children, two of-- three of my own and a neighbor girl, who had made a big hole for themselves to hide in when bombs came. When the airplanes came over, they all put their hats down, and they felt safe then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture during the war from people who were hidden with us and ourselves. The man with his arm up is my husband, Aart. And the man at the other side was a Jewish hider. His name was Kurt Delmonte. And the woman next to my husband was [? Ilona. ?] I don't remember her last name. And then one of the little Jewish girl is standing next to our son, Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the one with the dark hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's the one with the dark hair. And Peter stands next to her. And then there is Hetty and Barbara, my two daughters, and the little daughter of [? Ilona, ?] who is next to my husband here. And we are-- he is pointing up, and we all are looking at one of the English airplanes that came over our country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken in-- in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are some of the awards my husband and I got after the war from Jewish groups or organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get many awards from Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle book I wrote when I was 18 years old. And it was just a novel. And the right one was a book about the war, where I tell many of the things that had happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the title of that book?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? As a Cedar on the Lebanon ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what award did you win?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I won a literary award on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [INAUDIBLE]. That was in a publishing house. And it was an award from a group of publishing houses. And they had five prizes, and I won the second prize, to my amazement. And that other book I wrote, while I was reading that last book. It's just a silly book for young girls, you know, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that title mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The title means Muis and Luuk, that are two names of girls, go see [INAUDIBLE]. And that was just when I got stuck in one of the other books, I wrote a funny book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see she's hitchhiking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. And that didn't exist in Holland yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and I at the later ages, a few years before my husband died, which is six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the occasion for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was in the-- it was for the Kingston Freeman newspaper. And the photographer gave me pictures and they were in the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband name was Aart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aart.

speaker: interviewee
text: With two A's. A-A-R-T. And that's not short for Arthur or anything. That was just his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aart. I want to thank you, again, Mrs. Vos, for being so patient, for sharing this wonderful, wonderful story.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, you are very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure to do it with you.
